mnemonics: Think of a Russian nesting doll: To see the smallest one, you must keep opening the larger one until you find the base case, then you put them all back together.
keyPoints: A recursive method calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem.
Every recursive method must have a base case (a condition to stop) to prevent infinite recursion and a stack overflow error.
With each recursive call, the problem must be moving towards the base case (e.g., by decrementing a counter or processing a smaller sub-array).
commonMistakes: Forgetting the base case, leading to infinite recursion and a StackOverflowError.
Not ensuring the recursive call progresses toward the base case, also leading to infinite recursion.
Attempting to use recursion for problems with very large input sizes, which can cause a StackOverflowError due to deep call stacks; an iterative solution is often better here.
